Tafseer:
Alif-Lam-Mim-Ra. Detailed discussion on the abbreviated letters (Huroof-e-Muqatta'at) has already been presented at the beginning of Surah Al-Baqarah. In our view, these are the names of the chapters. Here, the subject (mubtada) is omitted, meaning: 'This is Alif-Lam-Mim-Ra.'

Simultaneous Expression of Favor and Warning: 'These are the verses of the Book.' Detailed discussion on the word 'al-Kitab' has been presented under the first verses of Surah Al-Baqarah. It refers to the promised and awaited divine Book which Allah promised to send down as the final scripture of guidance through His prophets and messengers. 'Tilka' (Those/These) points towards 'Alif-Lam-Mim-Ra'. That is to say, this Surah comprises verses of the promised and awaited divine Book. This phrase simultaneously contains an expression of favor as well as a threat. The favor is that Allah has sent down that great blessing which He had promised through His prophets and messengers. Now it is the duty of the people to appreciate it with a sincere heart and benefit from its blessings. The aspect of threat is that these are the verses of the divine Book; so those who, instead of appreciating them, make fun of them and expend their energy in opposing them, should think for themselves what doom they are inviting upon themselves.

Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): 'And that which has been revealed to you from your Lord is the truth, but most of the people do not believe.' This is consolation offered to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that this Book which has been revealed to you from your Lord is truth in its entirety. Every single claim of it is proven, reasoned, substantiated, and bound to happen. There is nothing in it that can be disputed; it is the deprivation and misfortune of the people themselves that the majority of them are not accepting it.

An Indication of God's Great Power and Wisdom: 'It is Allah Who raised the heavens without pillars that you can see.' The word 'amad' is the plural of both 'amood' and 'imad', and 'tarawnaha' (you see them) is its adjective. That is, God has erected the heavens without any such pillars that are visible to you. Allah has drawn attention to His great power and wisdom, showing that He has erected this great canopy on invisible pillars of gravity and attraction which your eyes are unable to see. 'Then He established Himself on the Throne.' That is, He did not just create them and sit aside in some corner as the polytheists assume; rather, He is established on His Throne of authority and is ruling over this entire universe. 'And He subjected the sun and the moon, each running [its course] for a specified term.' These two great signs, the sun and the moon, bear witness by their very existence that everything, at all times, is within the grasp of God's power. It is impossible for anything to deviate even a hair's breadth from its designated orbit. The times set for the rising and setting of the sun and the moon strictly adhere to this scheduled system; there is never a difference of even a minute or a second. 'He regulates the affair; He details the signs that you may, of the meeting with your Lord, be certain.' That is, these signs also prove that God alone is managing the entire universe, and at the same time, He is clarifying all the implications of these signs so that you may believe that just as there is a specified term for everything, there is also a specified term for you. Ultimately, one day you have to return to God and meet Him.

Indication of the Signs of the Earth After the Heaven: After the signs of the heaven, attention is drawn to the signs of the earth—that just as the signs of the heaven manifest His creation, governance, power, wisdom, and uniqueness, the same attributes are witnessed in every corner of the earth, provided there are thinking hearts and pondering minds. How different the rivers and mountains seem from each other in their nature, yet God, through His power and wisdom, has created such harmony between them that He made springs of water flow from within the stones. Is this proof that they came into existence on their own and are ruled by separate deities, or is it proof that one powerful and wise Ruler created all these opposites and then, through His endless wisdom, created a wondrous harmony among them, producing compatibility akin to pairs?

Testimony of the Signs of Heaven and Earth: The kind of contrast and, at the same time, the kind of compatibility found in every corner of this universe, within everything—heaven and earth, sun and moon, night and day—clearly testifies that this universe is not a battleground of deities with conflicting interests, but rather a single, self-sustaining Almighty's will is at work upon it.

Universality of the Law of Contrast and Compatibility: Through the words 'of all fruits', reference is made to the universality of this law of contrast and compatibility—that just as this law is at work within night and day, it is also at work within every single fruit and grain, whether man knows it or not. If you look at a grain of wheat, it too appears divided into two parts. However, there is complete connection and integration between both. This testimony from every corner of the universe indicates that this world is not alone, but it also has a partner, which is the Hereafter. Only by joining with its partner does it reach its ultimate purpose; otherwise, its existence would be completely meaningless and without an end.

Signs are Useful Only for the People of Truth: 'Indeed in that are signs for a people who give thought.' That is, within these things are many signs of God's creation, governance, power, wisdom, monotheism, and the Hereafter, but the intellect of only those people reaches these signs who ponder over them and make the guidance obtained from them the very core of their lives. Careless and indifferent people cannot derive any benefit from these things.

Indication of Another Aspect of the Signs of the Earth: This draws attention to another aspect of the signs of the earth: Do you see that there are neighboring tracts of land side by side? Some have gardens of grapevines, some have crops, and some have date palms, some growing from single roots and others from distinct roots. The plots are close to each other, all irrigated with the same water, yet they differ in the yield of their produce—some yielding more, some less. They also differ in taste—some are high in quality, while others are lower. There is also a major difference in the growth of the trees—sometimes several trunks spring from a single root, and sometimes only a single trunk emerges. Does this situation testify that all this is happening on its own and that a blind, deaf law of nature is imposed on everything, or does it testify that one all-knowing, wise God is running this entire system of the universe under His watch, ruling over the entire world of causes alone, and manipulating every particle of it according to His wisdom?

The Real Cause for Wonder: That is, in the presence of these signs of God's power, wisdom, creation, and governance mentioned above, the thing to wonder at is not what you (addressing the Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) are warning people of—that they will be raised after death and presented before God for accountability—but rather, what is truly astonishing is the wonder of these people themselves when they say: 'When we are dust, will we indeed be in a new creation?'

The Correct Meaning of Believing in God: 'Those are the ones who have disbelieved in their Lord.' It is stated that these are the actual deniers of their Lord. For believing in God, it is not sufficient for a person to merely accept that God exists; rather, it is necessary to believe in Him with His attributes and His rights. If a person acknowledges God but denies His obvious and necessary attributes, or the rights and requirements arising from them, then despite his acknowledgment, he is not a believer but a disbeliever. In the scale of God, his acknowledgment holds no weight. God is not in need of anyone's belief; rather, people are in need of believing in Him, and this benefit is achieved only by believing in Him correctly.

The Real Cause of the Deniers' Denial: 'And those will have shackles upon their necks.' The meaning is that around the necks of these people are the shackles of pride, ego, self-worship, blind following, and stagnation. These shackles neither allow their necks to lift upward so they can ponder the signs of heaven referenced in verse 2, nor do they let them bend down toward the signs of the earth referred to in verses 3 and 4. The result of this blindness is that they will fall into Hell and rot in it forever. This reality has been explained in Surah Yaseen: 'Indeed, We have put shackles on their necks, and they are to their chins, so they are with heads kept aloft.'

The Wise is One Who Learns a Lesson from Others: 'Mathulaat' is the plural of 'Mathulah'. It means punishment and exemplary chastisement. The meaning is that these people are waiting for some calamity or punishment before repenting and reforming. They say that when they see some sign of the punishment with which the Prophet is threatening them, only then will they believe. However, the wise person is one who learns a lesson from others. How many generations have passed before them who, like them, denied their respective prophets and ultimately met their fate? Their stories are being narrated to them. Is there no lesson of warning for them in those stories? It is only Allah's grace that He grants people a long respite for repentance and reform despite their disbelief and polytheism, but there is a limit to this respite as well. After it passes, when He seizes, His seizure is indeed very severe.

Answer to the Demand for Punishment: 'Ayah' (sign) here refers to some sign of punishment. This points to the same punishment for which they are showing haste in the previous verse, as they ask: 'Why does the Prophet not show us some sign of the punishment he constantly warns us about?' It is said that your duty is only to warn people of this punishment; showing some sign of it or bringing down the punishment is not your job. That is Our job. You do your work and leave Our work to Us. Do not care about their nonsense. 'And for every people is a guide' points to the divine sunnah (way) that Allah warns and admonishes a nation before punishing them. Thus, for their warning, God has sent you as a guide, which clearly means that if they do not accept your guidance, then after this, only the stage of punishment remains for them.

That is, to cast doubt on a matter which is a reality and bound to happen—simply because you cannot tell its exact time or show it upon their demand—is not a rational approach. A woman becomes pregnant; whether there is a boy or a girl in her womb, only Allah knows it. The decrease or increase that secretly occurs in it, Allah alone knows. The exact time of delivery is also in the knowledge of Allah alone. Not knowing these things does not negate the pregnancy itself, nor would any sensible person deny a pregnant woman's pregnancy on this basis. This is the very example of divine punishment for these wrongdoers. They have conceived its burden due to the corruption of their beliefs and deeds, and this burden will surely manifest when it reaches its term. But when it will come, and in what form, is known exactly to Allah alone; no one else has this knowledge. 'And everything with Him is by due measure.' With Allah, everything has fixed regulations, set measures, and appointed times. The divine sunnah that He has established for everything does not change due to people's haste.

[He is] Knower of the unseen and the witnessed, the Grand, the Exalted. This is a further explanation and emphasis of the subject matter above. The meaning is: Do not try to measure God's matters, His knowledge, and His plans with the limits of your own knowledge. His knowledge encompasses all that is hidden and present. He is a truly magnificent Being, and His presence is highly exalted. He alone knows the secrets of His intentions and His schemes; others can know only as much of it as He chooses to reveal.

It is the same [to Him] concerning you whether one conceals his speech or publicizes it, and whether one is hidden by night or goes freely by day. The meaning is that anxiety over sooner or later should be felt by one who fears that a slight delay will cause the opportunity to slip away, rendering him unable to control his opponent. Why should He make haste in seizing, whose knowledge and power encompass everything and everyone in such a way that both their secrets and public deeds are in His knowledge, and He has complete control over every movement they make by night and by day? Whenever He wills and from wherever He wills, He will seize anyone. Who has the power to escape His control or hide from Him anywhere?

'For him are successive [angels] before him and behind him who guard him by the decree of Allah.' 'Mu'aqqibat' means successive spiritual beings, referring to those angels who are appointed in turns by God to watch over every human being. The phrase 'by the decree of Allah' (min amrillah) is for clarification, just as in 'Say, the soul is from the decree of my Lord' (qulir-roohu min amri Rabbi) and in some other verses. That is, these angels or these souls are by the command of Allah.

Further Explanation of the Above Subject: These verses further explain the subject of the previous verse. That is, Allah is fully aware of every person's open and hidden states, as well as their night and day. He has appointed His angels over every person as guardians. These angels are from the command of Allah, constantly monitoring every word and deed of every person.

The Divine Sunnah Regarding Punishment: 'Indeed, Allah will not change the condition of a people until they change what is in themselves.' Now, the fundamental sunnah of Allah regarding the punishment of nations has been clarified: Allah does not change His way with a nation until that nation changes its own conduct. When a nation changes its own conduct and does not take heed despite warnings and admonitions, Allah sends upon them that punishment which no power can avert. The meaning is: Instead of demanding signs of punishment, evaluate your own conditions in the light of this divine sunnah. If you have changed your own conduct, then understand that there will be no delay in the changing of God's way with you which has been ongoing until now. Also, remember that when God decides to punish a nation, no one has the strength to avert His definitive decree. All your imaginary idols, fortresses, and bastions will turn to dust.

Verses 12-13: 'It is He who shows you the lightning, [causing] fear and aspiration, and generates the heavy clouds. And the thunder exalts [Allah] with praise of Him - and the angels [as well] from fear of Him - and He sends thunderbolts and strikes therewith whom He wills while they dispute about Allah; and He is severe in assault.'

Indication of Some Cosmic Signs: Now, in response to the demand for punishment that has been discussed above, attention is drawn to some cosmic signs: If there is a demand for signs, signs are manifested every day. Lightning flashes, carrying aspects of both hope and fear; it appears as a messenger of rain, and if Allah wills, He makes it a whip of punishment. Clouds rise, raining down as a shower of mercy, and if Allah wills, the flood of Noah can erupt from within them. After these signs, what other signs are you waiting for? In 'And the thunder exalts [Allah] with praise of Him - and the angels [as well] from fear of Him,' there is an implicit style of omission (hazf) to which we have pointed in more than one place: sometimes contrasting words are omitted because the context makes them clear. If we unfold this style, the entire statement would be: 'And the thunder exalts [Allah] with praise of Him from fear of Him, and the angels exalt Him with praise from fear of Him.' We have also clarified in another place that 'tasbeeh' (exaltation) is dominated by the aspect of declaring God free from flaws, while 'hamd' (praise) is the acknowledgment and confession of His beautiful attributes.

This is a further explanation of the above subject: While the audacity of the deniers and rejectors is such that they demand punishment, the state of the thunder, lightning, and angels is that they are constantly busy in His glorification and praise out of fear of Him, not knowing when what command might be issued. Then God sends His bolt of punishment upon whom He wills, while people are busy disputing about God. God is severe in assault, meaning He possesses great power; no one has the strength to block His strike.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 14 to 16: In the following few verses, this reality is clarified: those who invite God's punishment relying on their imaginary deities are merely inviting their own doom. God has no partner or associate. He is One, without any partner. Fruitful calling is calling upon God alone. Calling upon others besides Him is merely a cry in the wilderness. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'To Him is the supplication of truth. And those they call upon besides Him do not respond to them with a thing, except as one who stretches his hands toward water to reach his mouth, but it will not reach it. And the supplication of the disbelievers is not but in error.'

Fruitful Calling is Only Calling Upon God: 'To Him is the supplication of truth.' That is, fruitful, beneficial, and blessing-inducing calling is calling upon Allah alone, because all choice, authority, power, and influence belong to Him alone. Those who rely on other deities besides God, even challenging God Himself on their strength, calling upon them is merely a cry in the wilderness. Firstly, they have no real existence, and even if they do, they are completely helpless in helping them.

An Illustration of the Polytheists' Deprivation: Their deprivation and disappointment are compared to a thirsty person who, in the restlessness of his thirst, stretches both hands toward water that is beyond his reach. Just as he writhes in thirst and remains deprived of water, they too will strike themselves in their deprivation. The exact meaning of 'And the supplication of the disbelievers is not but in error' is what we express in our words as 'a cry in the wilderness.' This is, so to speak, the expression of the theme of 'To Him is the supplication of truth' in another style.

'And to Allah prostrates whoever is within the heavens and the earth, willingly or by compulsion, and their shadows in the mornings and the afternoons.'

Creation's Testimony of Everything: This is a further emphasis of the above subject through the physical testimony of everything. The meaning is that everything in its basic existence is submissive and obedient to God. Those who have been given some choice—even if they behave arrogantly by taking wrong advantage of this choice—are still forced to bow before God in their physical, created existence. This is testimony to the fact that our existence, by its very nature, is obedient to God. If we act arrogantly toward God, this state is, in reality, a rebellion against our own basic nature. This reality is clarified through this example: see, the shadow of everything lies stretched out before God morning and evening, and it does not raise its head from this physical prostration all night. In the morning, it slowly raises its head, and then, with the decline of the sun, the same state of bowing and prostrating begins to overcome it. This theme is also stated in verses 48-49 of Surah An-Nahl. If Allah wills, we will explain it in more detail there. This is a special type of argument which we term as 'indications' (isharaat). Although the scale of logic is unable to estimate the true value of these arguments, they carry great weight in the scale of human nature.

'Say, "Who is Lord of the heavens and the earth?" Say, "Allah." Say, "Have you then taken besides Him allies not possessing even for themselves any benefit or any harm?" Say, "Is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Or is darkness equivalent to light? Or have they attributed to Allah partners who created like His creation so that the creation [of each] seemed similar to them?" Say, "Allah is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the Prevailing."' 

'Say, "Who is Lord of the heavens and the earth?" Say, "Allah."' The word 'qul' (say) is used in the sense of 'tell', 'ask', 'question', and 'answer'. Its correct meaning has to be determined keeping in view the context and occasion. The meaning is: Ask them who is the owner of the heavens and the earth and the Lord of the universe? Then tell them that the owner and Lord of all of them is God alone. We have clarified in another place that the people of Arabia basically believed in Allah as the Creator and Owner of the heavens and the earth, but they had set up other allies and partners, assuming they were highly favored by God, and that worshipping them was a means to gain proximity to God. They believed all worldly blessings were obtained through their grace, and if any stage of the Hereafter were to arise, these partners would secure their forgiveness. 'Say, "Have you then taken besides Him allies not possessing even for themselves any benefit or any harm?"' That is, when the Owner of the heavens and the earth is He alone, ask them by what logic have they taken other guardians and protectors besides God, whose state is such that—far from bringing benefit to others or warding off harm from them—they are not even capable of bringing benefit to themselves or removing any calamity that befalls them.

The Meaning of the Blind and the Seeing: 'Say, "Is the blind equivalent to the seeing? Or is darkness equivalent to light?"' The words 'blind' and 'seeing' are used here in the sense of the intellectually and morally blind versus those who possess sight. Similarly, 'darkness' refers to intellectual and moral darkness, while 'light' refers to the light of intellect and faith. In using the plural for 'darkness' (zulumat) while using the singular for the contrasting 'light' (noor), there is a subtle point: the paths and doors through which intellectual and moral evils manifest can be, and are, diverse, but the door to intellectual and moral light is only one, and that is Allah.

An Ax to the Root of Polytheism: Now, an ax is taken to the root of polytheism: you assume partners to God, believing they will save their worshippers from God's seizure regardless of their actions, deeds, beliefs, and ideas. In other words, this means that in the sight of God, the blind and the seeing, and darkness and light, are both equal. In this way, you have demolished the very foundation of truth and justice upon which these heavens and earth are established, and without which this entire universe would be reduced to a place of darkness or a playground for amusement.

On the Basis of Which Argument is Polytheism Practiced? 'Or have they attributed to Allah partners...?' That is, on the basis of what proof have they made partners to God? Are there, among the creation, some things created by their assumed partners which have caused them this confusion, making them unable to determine what is God's creation and what is their partners' creation? 'Say, "Allah is the Creator of all things, and He is the One, the Prevailing."' The meaning is that the Creator of everything is Allah alone, and you too do not deny this reality; so on what basis have you associated others in God's creation? We have discussed the word 'Al-Qahhar' (the Prevailing/Subduing) in another place, showing its correct meaning is 'the One Who keeps everyone under His control'. The meaning is that He is the Creator and is also capable of keeping everything under His control, so what is the necessity on the basis of which other partners should be associated in His divinity?

Subsequent Subject. Verses 17 to 26: In the following, further clarification is given to the reality pointed out above—that polytheism demolishes the very foundation of truth and justice on which the system of this world is established, and to which the very nature of this universe bears witness. Also, a very subtle indication is made to the reality that the revelation of the Qur'an has created a conflict between truth and falsehood which will ultimately end in the victory of the truth, while falsehood will vanish like foam. Read the subsequent verses in this light:

'He sends down from the sky, rain, and valleys flow according to their capacity, and the torrent carries a rising foam. And from that [ore] which they heat in the fire, desiring ornaments or utensils, is a foam like it. Thus Allah presents the truth and falsehood. As for the foam, it vanishes, [being] cast off; but as for that which benefits the people, it remains on the earth. Thus Allah presents examples.'

In verse 16, it was pointed out that the most hideous aspect of polytheism and false intercession is that it treats the blind and the seeing, light and darkness, or in other words, truth and falsehood, as equal—which is clearly against intellect and justice. Now, taking a step forward, it is clarified that this is also against the nature of this universe and the divine sunnah which is active and implemented in it.

Testimony of the Nature of the Universe: The nature of this universe is explained thus: you see that when rain falls from the sky, the valleys and streams overflow and flow, then you see that the torrent carries the foam up to the surface. Subsequently, the foam vanishes into the air like dry rubbish, but the water, which is beneficial for the earth and its inhabitants, remains in the earth.

Then the same reality is explained with another example: when you melt silver in a crucible to make some ornament or other article, its dross comes to the top and is discarded, while the pure silver remains.

The Survival of the Beneficial in the Conflict of Truth and Falsehood: From both these examples, the temperament of this universe created by God becomes clear: it actually wants to retain what is beneficial and constantly filters out what is useless. Allah has based His sunnah on this very principle: when He clashes truth with falsehood, the foam of falsehood that rises above the truth in this clash vanishes like dry rubbish, while the truth, which is beneficial to people, remains. The meaning is: in the same way, the rain that has fallen on the earth at this time in the form of the Qur'an has also raised some foam to the surface, but all this foam will perish, while the Qur'an and its bearers will remain. 'Thus Allah presents examples'—the segment after this is omitted. The meaning is: Allah is presenting these parables so that those who are standing up in support of falsehood may see their future in them, and the believers may also see their bright future.

'For those who have responded to their Lord is the best [outcome], but those who did not respond to Him - if they had all that is in the earth entirely and the like of it with it, they would [attempt to] ransom themselves thereby. Those will have the worst account, and their refuge is Hell, and wretched is the destination.'

The Actual Reality in Simple Words: The described noun for 'Al-Husna' (the best) is omitted here as it is clear from the context; meaning, 'the best outcome' (Al-Aaqibah Al-Husna). What was stated above in a metaphorical style is here put in simple words: those who are responding today to the call which the Messenger of Allah is presenting in the form of the Qur'an, their end is good. As for those who are evading or opposing it, let them remember that when their end comes before them, their state at that time will be such that if they possessed the entire world and another like it, they would want to give all of it as ransom to escape. It is said that these are the ones for whom is the worst account, their abode will be Hell, and it is a wretched abode. 'The worst account' (soo' al-hisab) points to the reality that they will be gripped for every single small thing, and their every word and deed will appear in its original, terrifying form. As for the believers, oversight will be shown regarding their slips and shortcomings, but as for these arrogant ones, if even a grain of their deeds is weighed, it will stand before them like a mountain. 'If they had all that is in the earth entirely...' sheds light on the actual cause of their aversion and denial: it is the love of this world and its provisions that is preventing them from accepting the call of the Qur'an. But when the end of this world, with which they are infatuated today, comes before them, all this infatuation will vanish. At that time, they will wish that if they possessed this entire world and another like it, they would give it all as ransom to achieve salvation.

'Then is he who knows that what has been revealed to you from your Lord is the truth like one who is blind? They will only be reminded who are people of understanding.'

Consolation to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), Warning to the Deniers: The word 'blind' (a'ma), just as it came in verse 16 for the blind of intellect and heart, has also been used in this verse for the blind of intellect and heart. This verse, by way of transition (iltifat), serves as consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and as a warning and threat for the deniers and rejectors. The aspect of consolation for the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is that everyone's attitude will not be the same regarding the call you are presenting in the form of the Qur'an. Only those who possess intellect and heart (Ulu'l-Albab) will take heed from it. Those whose hearts are dead and whose intellects have gone blind will not derive any benefit from it. The meaning is: do not be disheartened and worried over their attitude without reason.

The style of this verse speaks for itself—showing that people are of two types: one are those for whom every word of the Prophet and the Qur'an is the very voice of their own heart. Upon hearing the words of the Prophet and the verses of the Qur'an, they feel as if:

'Look at the pleasure of the speech, that what he said
I felt as if it was already in my heart.'

For them, the Qur'an acts as a reminder. It does not impose anything on them from the outside, but rather unearths the buried treasures of their own nature and places them before their eyes.

The warning in it for the deniers and rejectors is: how is it possible that such blind people should become like those whose hearts and minds are illuminated? It is necessary that the end of both should be different according to their deeds.

'Those who fulfill the covenant of Allah and do not break the contract.'

The Meaning of 'Covenant' (Meethaq): 'Covenant' here refers to the primordial covenant of human nature (Meethaq-e-Fitrat) which was taken from all the children of Adam, and which everyone acknowledged. A detailed discussion on this has passed under Surah Al-A'raf, verse 172: 'And when your Lord took from the children of Adam - from their loins - their descendants and made them testify of themselves, [saying to them], "Am I not your Lord?" They said, "Yes, we have testified."' The meaning is that those who remember this covenant and have not broken this pledge are the ones who will take heed from this Qur'an. As for those who have corrupted their nature and broken this covenant, do not expect any good from them.

'And those who join that which Allah has ordered to be joined and fear their Lord and dread the evil account.'

Respecting the Ties of Relationship: 'That which Allah has ordered to be joined' refers to respecting and maintaining the ties of kinship (silah rahmi). That is, just as they sincerely respect the rights of Allah, they also generously fulfill the rights of the servants of Allah that are binding upon them by virtue of kinship. They do not sever the ties of relationship but join them according to the command of Allah. They do all this not for show, vanity, or personal interest, but out of fear of God and to seek His pleasure. This reality has been explained in Surah Ad-Dahr as well: 'And they give food in spite of love for it to the needy, the orphan, and the captive, [saying], "We feed you only for the countenance of Allah. We wish not from you reward or gratitude. Indeed, we fear from our Lord a harsh, distressful Day."'

'And those who are patient, seeking the countenance of their Lord, and establish prayer and spend from what We have provided for them, secretly and publicly, and prevent evil with good - those will have the consecutive home.'

More of their attributes are being described—that whatever difficulties and calamities they face in the way of fulfilling the rights of Allah and His servants, they bear them for the sake of seeking Allah's pleasure. 'They establish prayer' means they maintain the prayer. We have clarified in other places that there is a big difference between merely performing prayer and maintaining it. See its details in its respective place.

The Importance of Secret Spending: 'And spend from what We have provided for them, secretly and publicly.' Its regular form is Zakat, but this does not mean that the command of general charity (infaq) was annulled after the command of Zakat. Zakat falls under public spending. Secret spending, on which the real virtue depends, remained even after Zakat, and from the perspective of self-purification, the primary importance belongs to this secret spending. 'And prevent evil with good'—that is, they do not counter evil with evil, but rather repel it with goodness and virtue. Although this is a comprehensive general principle, it has a specific context: in dealing with those who deserve their help, they do not look at how that person has behaved toward them, but rather they behave well even toward those who have behaved badly toward them. It is proven from both the Qur'an and the Hadith that this virtue carries great weight in the scale of Allah. 'Those will have the consecutive home (Uqba'd-dar).' 'Dar' refers to the home of the Hereafter. It is stated that the actual reward and recompense of the home of the Hereafter is indeed for these people. This means that the assumption of those who have invented the belief in polytheism and false intercession—treating truth and falsehood, justice and oppression as equal—is completely baseless.

Verses 23-24: 'Gardens of perpetual residence; they will enter them with whoever were righteous among their fathers, their spouses and their descendants. And the angels will enter upon them from every gate, [saying], "Peace be upon you for what you patiently endured. And excellent is the consecutive home."' 

Regard for Emotions in Paradise: This is the detail of 'Uqba'd-dar' (the good end) mentioned in the previous verse. It is said that for them will be gardens of eternity which they will enter, and to complete their happiness, those among their forefathers, spouses, and descendants who qualify by virtue of their deeds will also be gathered with them. It is human nature that when one is blessed with a favor, one's heartfelt desire is that those who have been dear to them should also share in it. Having regard for this requirement of human nature, Allah will gather their relatives and near ones with them, provided they are fit to enter Paradise. This condition is a fundamental condition; if it is not kept in view, the system of justice discussed in these verses would be destabilized. But this condition does not negate Allah's grace that He, to complete the happiness of these righteous and pious people, will gather with them those of their relatives who, though lower in rank and degree, are still among those deserving of Paradise. 'And the angels will enter upon them...'—the previous point was for the completion of happiness, and now the aspect of their honor and respect is highlighted: this Paradise will have many gates, and from every gate, angels will come to them to offer peace and greetings, congratulating them on their steadfastness, in return for which they became deserving of this grace of Allah.

Appreciation from Allah: In our view, the phrase 'And excellent is the consecutive home' is not part of the angels' greetings; rather, it is appreciation from Allah for this high status. Above in verse 22, it was said: 'Those will have the consecutive home.' Now, when the glory and grandeur of this 'consecutive home' has been clarified, it is said by way of appreciation: 'And excellent is the consecutive home.' See, how excellent indeed is the success of the home of the Hereafter!

'And those who break the covenant of Allah after its contract and sever that which Allah has ordered to be joined and spread corruption on earth - for them is the curse, and they will have the worst home.'

Attributes of the Deniers: Now, this is the description of the completely opposite group: those who break the covenant of human nature after acknowledging and binding it, and who sever the ties of kinship—the severing of which is the root of all corruption on earth—which Allah has ordered to be joined. It is stated that these are the ones upon whom is the curse of Allah, and for them is the humiliation and disgrace of the home of the Hereafter. We have clarified in another place that the Islamic system of truth and justice is established on two basic pillars: one is the oneness of God, and the second is the oneness of humanity. If both these foundations are demolished, then the existence of a righteous society and a righteous civilization becomes impossible.

'Allah extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]. And they rejoice in the worldly life, while the worldly life is not, compared to the Hereafter, but [brief] enjoyment.'

The actual structure of the sentence is: 'He extends provision for whom He wills, and He restricts it for whom He wills.' Due to the clarity of the context, 'for whom He wills' was omitted in one place. 'Qadara' means to measure, but here it is in the sense of 'to restrict'. The indefiniteness of the word 'mata' (brief enjoyment) is proof of its insignificance.

Clarification of the Real Cause of Denial: Now, the real cause of the denial and rejection of these deniers is being clarified: they have been spoiled by pride and arrogance due to the worldly blessings they have received, and the reminder of the Hereafter from the Prophet is heavy upon them. However, it is Allah Who opens the doors of sustenance for whom He wills and restricts them for whom He wills. From the one for whom He extends it, He desires that he become a grateful servant of his Lord; and from the one for whom He restricts it, He desires that he be patient. The entire building of religion is established on this very patience and gratitude. Those who, having received the worthless pebbles of this world, have forgotten the Hereafter in their arrogance, will realize when they see the reward of the grateful and patient on the Last Day that they lost an eternal kingdom for an extremely trivial thing.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 27 to 32: Now, this reality is being clarified: these arguments of the cosmos and the soul being narrated to them are not bringing them peace. They are demanding that some physical miracle be shown to them, only then will they believe that he is the Messenger of Allah and that what he says is the truth. In response to this demand of theirs, it is stated that the path of faith and guidance opens by pondering over the words of Allah and His Messenger. Those who do not adopt this path remain blind even after seeing all the miracles of the world. Observe the verses:

'And those who disbelieved say, "Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?" Say, "Indeed, Allah leaves astray whom He wills and guides to Himself whoever turns back [to Him]."'

The Guide of Faith is the Signs of the Cosmos and the Soul: 'Sign' (Ayah) here refers to some physical miracle. That is, these people are keeping their eyes closed to the signs of the cosmos and the soul that surround them from all sides, and to which the Qur'an is drawing their attention, but they keep demanding that the Prophet show them a miracle before they believe he is the Messenger of Allah. In response, Allah reminded them of His sunnah—that He does not grant His guidance to those who demand miracles and wonders; rather, He grants this blessing to those who, seeing the signs of the cosmos and the soul, become seekers of truth and turn to God for clarity of mind and peace of heart. For those in whom this quest is born, 'the face and voice of the Prophet is itself a miracle.' As for those who remain after wonders and tricks, their lives are always spent stumbling. Allah wills the same for those who remain blind despite having eyes—that they remain in their misguidance. If God wanted to make people believers by force and through miracles, He has miracles greater than one another, but He wants people to use their intellect and thought and turn to Him.

'Those who have believed and whose hearts are assured by the remembrance of Allah. Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured.'

This is the detail of 'whoever turns back [to Him]', and 'remembrance of Allah' (zikrullah) refers to the description of Allah's attributes and His arguments and proofs present in the Qur'an. The meaning is that Allah guides to Himself only those whose hearts find peace in Allah's attributes, arguments, and proofs. Those who do not adopt this path remain deprived of faith and keep demanding miracles.

Opposition in the Remembrance of Allah: 'Unquestionably, by the remembrance of Allah hearts are assured'—now, this actual point is stated with full emphasis: listen carefully, if peace of heart, faith, and clarity of mind are desired, they are not achieved through miracles and wonders, but are achieved through the remembrance of and reflection on Allah and His attributes of beauty and perfection. So, if this is what is desired, listen to the call of the Prophet and ponder over it; otherwise, stumble in whatever valley you wish.

'Those who have believed and done righteous deeds - to them is bliss and a good place of return.' The meaning is that those who adopted this path, and by adopting it, walked upon the highway of faith and righteous deeds, for them is congratulation and glad tidings; they will be blessed with a good destination and a good abode. As for those who are waiting for miracles, they will one day face their end in this very state of waiting, which is destined for such blind of intellect.

'Thus have We sent you to a community before which other communities have passed on so you might recite to them that which We revealed to you, while they disbelieve in the Most Merciful. Say, "He is my Lord; there is no deity except Him. Upon Him I rely, and to Him is my return."' 

Answer to the Demand for Miracles from Another Perspective: This is also an answer to the same demand for miracles mentioned in verse 27. The meaning is: neither are you the first prophet in the world, nor are they (the Arabs) the first nation in the world. Prophets and messengers have passed before you, and nations and communities have passed before them. For you, there is an example and model in the lives of your predecessor prophets and messengers; and for them, there is a precedent and example in the lives of their predecessor nations. So, do not be grieved by their constantly new demands, but recite to them this Book of Allah which is being revealed to you, even if they continue to deny the Merciful God. In response to their demands, declare to them: 'Allah is my Lord, He has no partner, in Him alone I trust, and to Him is my return. I am reciting to you the message He has sent me with. Showing miracles is not my job; it depends on His wisdom and will. If He wills, He will show them; if He does not, He will not.'

'And if there was a Qur'an by which mountains could be moved or the earth could be cloven asunder or the dead could be made to speak... Nay, to Allah belongs the affair entirely. Have those who believed not accepted that had Allah willed, He would have guided all of mankind? And those who disbelieve do not cease to be struck, for what they have done, by calamity, or it settles close to their home until there comes the promise of Allah. Indeed, Allah does not fail in His promise.'

In 'And if there was a Qur'an... or the dead could be made to speak,' the answer to the conditional clause is omitted. The meaning is: they do demand a miracle, and from this, a well-meaning person might assume that perhaps they would believe upon seeing a miracle of their choice. But this assumption is merely an assumption. Even if such a Qur'an had been revealed to them by which mountains moved, or the earth clove asunder, or the dead spoke, they still would not have believed; rather, even then, they would have made some excuse to stick to their disbelief. After all, how many miracles did their predecessors see, yet they called them magic and trickery and remained obstinate in their disbelief until the divine punishment finally decided their fate? 'Nay, to Allah belongs the affair entirely.' The meaning is: leave this matter entirely to Allah. He alone knows what they deserve and what treatment should be meted out to them. He will deal with them according to His sunnah regarding guidance and misguidance. He will open the way of guidance only for those who prove worthy of it according to His law.

Consolation to the Muslims: 'Have those who believed not accepted that had Allah willed, He would have guided all of mankind?' This offers consolation to the pure-hearted Muslims: a desire arose in you that they should be shown a miracle according to their demand, but is this not enough for your peace of mind that if Allah had wanted to bring people to the path of guidance through His absolute power, He could have made everyone a submissive believer in an instant? But He did not do so because He wants people to use their intellect and insight, ponder over the proofs of the cosmos and the soul, think and understand the words of the Prophet, and then choose this path of truth with the freedom of their choice and will.

Indication to the Warnings: 'And those who disbelieve do not cease to be struck... Indeed, Allah does not fail in His promise.' That is, it is not the case that signs are not appearing; from time to time, as a retribution for their misdeeds, warnings keep descending upon them or upon the people around them. The purpose of these is that these people should wake up, open their eyes, and listen to and understand the words of the Prophet, but they do not learn any lesson from them. Instead, they are waiting for the whip of divine punishment to fall upon their own backs before they believe. So, its time will also come. The punishment with which they are being threatened will surely come if they remain obstinate. Allah will not fail in His promise. 'Until there comes the promise of Allah' refers to that divine punishment of which every messenger warned his nation—that if they did not listen to the messenger, divine punishment would ultimately come upon them and completely destroy them. Regarding the era of the messengers' advent, we have written elsewhere that during their time, there is a specific abundance of the manifestation of such signs that produce awakening among the heedless so that their ears open to hear the words of the messenger.

'And already were messengers ridiculed before you, but I granted respite to those who disbelieved; then I seized them, and how [terrible] was My penalty.'

The Wisdom Behind Delaying the Punishment: Now, the wisdom is clarified as to why—despite their stubbornness and their demands for punishment—the punishment is not coming upon them: it is only because Allah is giving them respite so that His argument may be fully established against them. It is said: messengers before you were also ridiculed by their nations, but despite this, We gave them respite, then We seized them—and see how We seized them and how painful Our punishment was! The same will happen to them. 'Iqab' is originally 'Iqabi'. Due to the rhythm of the verse, the 'Ya' was dropped. The kasrah remains as its trace.

Subsequent Subject. Verses 33 to 37: In the following, the reality is clarified: if this audacity of theirs is on the strength of their assumed deities—whom they have presumed as partners to God and as their helpers and protectors without any proof—then tell them that these deities have no existence at all. These are merely imaginary ideas. These people will face punishment in this world, and the punishment they are to face in the Hereafter is even more severe than this. Only His believing and monotheistic servants will be deserving of Paradise. If these people are denying this obvious reality, do not grieve over it; those among the People of the Book who are good and stand by their Book are very happy with this Book which has been revealed to you. Read the verses:

'Then is He who is a maintainer of every soul, [knowing] what it has earned...'

Omission for Eloquence: In this sentence, half of the question is omitted for the sake of eloquence. What is meant to be said is: Is the One Who is monitoring the deeds of every soul and Who is the One to account for every word and deed—is it He to Whom you are associating partners from among things that have no reality, no existence, and possess no knowledge or authority? This omission expresses the speaker's disgust and aversion toward this senseless concept. It is as if even mentioning this ugly idea is unacceptable to Him. 'And they attributed to Allah partners. Say, 'Name them.'' That is, they have made partners to Allah; tell them to name those whom they have made partners to Him. The anger and contempt implied in 'Say, 'Name them'' is obvious. Clearly, the polytheists in response could only take the names of those idols they worshipped: Hubal, Lat, Uzza, Na'ilah, etc. But without waiting for their response, their very existence was negated: 'Or do you inform Him of that which He does not know upon the earth or of an obvious show of words?'—Are you giving God news of things whose existence in the earth He is unaware of, or are you just talking in the air? In 'Or do you inform Him of that which He does not know...', there is a linguistic style which we have mentioned in another place as 'negating a thing by negating its necessary consequence'—that is, sometimes a thing is negated by negating its necessary consequence. Obviously, if a thing exists in this world, it is necessary that the Creator of this world should be aware of it. If the Creator Himself is not aware of a thing, what is the meaning of its existence? On this principle, Allah has asked the polytheists sarcastically: Are you rising to add to God's knowledge, or are you just talking in the air without any head or tail?

Supporting Polytheism for Worldly Interests: 'Rather, their plan has been made attractive to those who disbelieve...' This clarifies the actual reality at the end of the verses: those who are standing up in support of polytheism are doing so merely as a trick to save their authority and leadership, which is very dear to them at this time. The arguments of the Qur'an and the call of the Prophet have exposed the reality of polytheism in such a way that it no longer holds any weight even in the eyes of its greatest supporters. The question is purely of interest; hereditary seats of leadership are not easily abandoned, and for this reason—despite knowing that the path of God is the one to which the Qur'an is calling—they are sticking to their misguidance. Such people who knowingly deny the truth and support falsehood fall under the grip of God's law; and when someone falls under the grip of God's law, who has the power to bring them to the path of guidance?

'For them is punishment in the worldly life, and the punishment of the Hereafter is more severe. And they will not have from Allah any protector.' For such people, there is punishment in this world, and the punishment of the Hereafter is far greater than the punishment of this world. These people, who rely on their assumed partners and intercessors to save them from God's seizure, will find that none of them will be able to protect them. Here, remember the divine sunnah to which we have referred repeatedly: when the argument is fully established against a nation through a messenger, if they do not believe, they are surely destroyed.

'The description of Paradise, which the righteous are promised, is that beneath it rivers flow. Its fruit is eternal, and its shade. That is the consecutive end of those who fear Allah, and the consecutive end of the disbelievers is the Fire.'

Description of the End of the Righteous: In 'Its fruit is eternal, and its shade,' the predicate of 'its shade' is omitted due to the clarity of the context, meaning: 'and its shade is eternal.' According to the principle of contrast, after describing the end of the rejectors of the Messenger, the end of those who believed in the Messenger out of their fear of God is also described. It is said that they will be admitted into such evergreen gardens whose produce is eternal and whose shade is also eternal. That is, they will never know any autumn.

'And those to whom We have given the Book rejoice at what has been revealed to you, but among the alliances are those who deny part of it. Say, 'I have only been commanded to worship Allah and not associate [anything] with Him. To Him I invite, and to Him is my return.' 

The Attitude of the Righteous Among the People of the Book: 'And those to whom We have given the Book' refers to the good among the People of the Book, particularly the Christians, as detailed under verses 83-84 of Surah Al-Ma'idah. There were people who, based on the prophecies in their scriptures, were waiting for the Final Messenger. For this reason, when they saw the signs of confirmation of those prophecies in your call and the Book you brought, they welcomed it with great passion and joy. This pious group among the People of the Book has been mentioned in several places in the Qur'an, and generally, they are mentioned in a positive light, as is done here. The mention of these people here is intended to console the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him)—that if ignorant, stubborn people are mocking you, why should you care when a group among the bearers of the Scripture is welcoming you? It is also to encourage the deniers—showing that it is a pity if you deny that which the good and pious people of knowledge are welcoming, thereby depriving yourselves of its blessings.

An Indication Toward the Opponents: 'But among the alliances are those who deny part of it.' 'Alliances' (Al-Ahzab) here refers to the opposing parties: the Jews, the Christians, and the polytheists of Arabia. The nature of their opposition was also generally not such that they disagreed with every part of the Qur'an. After all, how could they disagree with every part of it? The disagreement between the teachings of the Qur'an and the teachings of the Torah and the Gospel basically lies in those places where the People of the Book made alterations. The polytheists of Arabia also basically disagreed with the Qur'an's call to monotheism; for other things, there was no reason for them to disagree either. The disagreement of the followers of Paul among the Christians was also based on their polytheism. Pointing to this disagreement of theirs, the Qur'an directed the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to announce to those whose basis of disagreement is monotheism: 'I worship only the One Allah and do not associate anyone with Him; my call is toward Him, and my return is to Him alone.' That is, I am not under the illusion that I can get help from any other protector or intercessor. This clear announcement was made so that if any of these parties hoped to put pressure to make the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) yield or become intimidated, they would abandon this vain hope. In 'And to Him is my return' (Wa ilayhi ma'ab), the structure of the sentence is exactly the same as in 'My penalty' (Iqab) above.

'And thus We have revealed it as an Arabic judgment. And if you should follow their inclinations after what has come to you of knowledge, you would not have against Allah any ally or any protector.'

This Qur'an is an Obligatory Command: 'Kazaalik' (Thus) in Arabic also comes in the sense in which we use the words 'therefore' or 'for this reason'. Here, it is in the sense of 'for this reason'. In the previous verse, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was commanded: 'Say, 'I have only been commanded to worship Allah and not associate [anything] with Him. To Him I invite, and to Him is my return.' Pointing to this, it is said: for the call and propagation of this very command, We have revealed this Book to you. 'An Arabic judgment'—this clarifies the status of this Book: it is a 'judgment' (Hukm), meaning a decisive verdict and an obligatory command, and it is in Arabic so that it is a favor as well as the completion of proof for all those whose language is Arabic. The meaning is: this is not a request or an appeal, but an obligatory command to be obeyed, both for you and for the Arabs. If it is not obeyed, then after a period of respite has passed, all those who refuse to obey it will face the same end that the deniers and opponents of the messengers faced. We have clarified in its respective place that the messenger sent to a nation stands as the court of Allah for them. He does not come to people with a request, but with the command of God, the obedience of which is obligatory, and the disobedience of which makes one deserving of punishment.

Address to the Prophet, Warning to the Polytheists: 'And if you should follow their inclinations after what has come to you of knowledge...' 'Inclinations' (Ahwa') refers to 'innovations' (Bida'at), as we have clarified in various places, because the foundation of innovations is not on knowledge but on desires. Here, specifically, it points to the polytheistic innovations of the Arabs. 'Knowledge' (Al-Ilm) refers to the correct knowledge which was revealed from Allah in the form of the Qur'an. Addressing the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it is said: after the revelation of this Arabic judgment and correct knowledge, if you follow the polytheistic innovations of these polytheists, no one will be able to save you from the grip of God. Although the address in this verse is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in terms of words, the warning in it is directed toward all the polytheists. The eloquence of addressing the Prophet is to make the listeners well aware that when even the Prophet cannot escape God's grip if he violates this command, what can be said of others!

Subsequent Subject. Verses 38 to 43: Conclusion of the Surah: The following verses serve as the conclusion of the Surah. The Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is consoled: do not be worried by the constantly new objections and demands for miracles from these opponents. Keep doing your work and leave the matter of the demand for miracles to Us. These people cannot see it, but the signs of the dominance of truth are beginning to appear in this very land. The word of God is bound to happen, and no one can stop His decision. Those who walked in the same way, employing similar plots, have passed before. God will unravel all the threads of their plots, and soon it will become clear to them who gets the success of the final end. If they do not accept you as a messenger, say: 'The testimony of Allah and the bearers of the knowledge of the Book is sufficient between me and you.' Read the verses:

'And We have already sent messengers before you and assigned to them wives and descendants. And it was not for any messenger to cause a sign to come except by permission of Allah. For every term is a decree.'

Answers to Two Objections: In this verse, answers to two objections of the opponents are given without explicitly quoting them. One is: he claims to be a messenger of Allah; if Allah had to send a messenger, would He send a human being like us? He has such a large army of angels; why did He not select one of them for this office? The second is: he is threatening us that if we do not accept his word, Allah's punishment will come upon us. If he is truthful in this claim, why does he not show us some sign of punishment so that it becomes clear to us that indeed, if we do not accept his word, divine wrath will fall upon us?

The answer to the first of these objections is: you are not the first messenger in the world; many messengers have come before you. All of them were human; none of them was superhuman. All had wives and children, and ate and drank just as you eat and drink. The answer to the second objection is: showing any sign or miracle is not within the power of the messenger, but it depends on Allah's permission and His wisdom. Allah has appointed a time for everything under His wisdom, and there is a decree for every appointed hour.

'Allah eliminates what He wills or confirms, and with Him is the Mother of the Book.' 'And confirms' means 'He confirms what He wills.'

All authority over this decree belongs to Allah alone. Every elimination and confirmation, and every exclusion and entry in it, occurs solely under His wisdom and will; no one else has any say in it, and the original Book is with Him alone, to which no one else has access.

'And whether We show you part of what We promise them or take you in death, upon you is only the delivery [of the message], and upon Us is the account.'

Punishment's Time is According to God's Will and Wisdom: That is, the punishment with which We are threatening them in the event of their denial of you—it is possible that We show you a sample of it in your lifetime, or We cause you to die and make them taste it after your death. The entire occurrence of this depends on Our will and wisdom. You have no say in this matter, nor should you be worried about it. The responsibility upon you is only to convey Our message to these people; taking them to account and reckoning is Our job.

'Have they not seen that We set upon the land, reducing it from its borders? And Allah decides; there is no adjuster of His decision. And He is swift in account.'

'The Land' Refers to Makkah: 'The land' (Al-Ard) here refers to the land of Makkah. The meaning is: if they want a sign of the dominance of Islam and their own defeat, why do they not look at this physical reality—that the call of Islam is gradually conquering the surroundings of Makkah and moving toward Makkah itself, and the day is not far when it will bring this land under its sway? Allah has described the gradual rise of Islam and its spread among the tribes around Makkah and in Madinah as His advance toward Makkah, and this description is a reality. The call of Islam began in Makkah, but the Quraysh bent all their power and every kind of oppression to suppress it. However, the voice of truth is not suppressed. Gradually, many people from the surrounding tribes entered Islam, and in Madinah, it flourished so much that ultimately Allah blessed this city with the honor of being the home of migration (Dar-ul-Hijrah). During this period of the call, as mentioned above, people who spoke well of Islam emerged even among the People of the Book. In short, although Islam remained oppressed in its own home during this period, it was flourishing outside, and tribe after tribe was leaving the leadership of Quraysh and their polytheistic religion to enter the obedience of Islam. Pointing to this situation, it is said: if the disbelievers want a sign, why do they not ponder over this sign? 'There is no adjuster of His decision. And He is swift in account'—that is, this dominance of Islam is a divine decree; it is bound to happen, and no one has the power to avert this definitive decree. Moreover, there is no much delay in it now. Soon that time is coming when this entire account will be settled.

The very same subject has been stated in Surah Al-Anbiya as well. Keep that verse in view too. Pointing to the Quraysh, it was said: 'Nay, We gave worldly enjoyment to these and their fathers until life was prolonged for them. Then do they not see that We set upon the land, reducing it from its borders? Is it they who will overcome?' (Al-Anbiya: 44). Islam's Dawah Victories: Probably based on this verse, some people have classified this Surah as Madani. Their mind went toward the idea that 'We reduce it from its borders' refers to the military victories of the Muslims, and since military victories belong to the Madani period, this Surah, in their view, is Madani. But in our view, this refers to the victories of Islam's propagation (dawah). Furthermore, you saw above that the very same verse, with a slight change of words, is also in Surah Al-Anbiya, which is unanimously Makki. However, from these words, it certainly emerges that this Surah belongs to that period of Makki life when the call of Islam was gaining strength among the tribes around Makkah and in Madinah, and the joints of Quraysh's leadership were loosening—particularly since their oppression of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the weak Muslims had completely degraded their moral standing in the eyes of those outside Makkah.

'And those before them had already planned, but to Allah belongs the plan entirely. He knows what every soul earns, and the disbelievers will know to whom belongs the consecutive home.'

Now, it is said: the kind of plots they are employing against Islam and the Prophet, similar plots were employed by nations before them against their respective prophets, and they have seen the end of it. These people are also going to see the same end. All planning is within the control of God; He is fully aware of what every soul does. Nothing is outside His control and knowledge. 'And the disbelievers will know to whom belongs the consecutive home'—that is, their end in this world has been told in the verses above; they will also soon know the end they are to face in the Hereafter—that to whom belongs the success and victory of the Hereafter.

'And those who have disbelieved say, 'You are not a messenger.' Say, 'Sufficient is Allah as a witness between me and you, and whoever has knowledge of the Book.' 

That is, if—after hearing and seeing all these proofs and testimonies—these disbelievers still say that you are not sent by God, then there is no need for further discussion with them. Tell them: the testimony of God is sufficient between me and you, as well as the testimony of those who are the true bearers of the knowledge of the divine Book. This points to those scholars among the People of the Book who were mentioned above in verse 36.

These are the last lines in the commentary of this Surah that were committed to paper by the pen of this humble writer. And our last call is that all praise belongs to Allah, the Lord of the worlds.
